title: Page Two: Key risks for banks as outlook sours
full_text:
Two pressing challenges confront the nation's biggest banks, and both are intrinsically linked to slower credit growth and tempering economic activity that will persist into next year.
The first is the risk that slower home loan growth, exacerbated by the federal budget's negative gearing and capital gains tax changes, triggers an aggressive pricing war in the mortgage market. And anecdotal evidence suggests this may have already begun.
The second challenge centres on how businesses navigate a slowing economy, volatility in oil prices and higher interest rates. Over the weekend, Iran again closed the Strait of Hormuz amid an escalation in hostilities, signalling the conflict in the Middle East has a way to run.
For bank investors, the fear is that the four biggest domestic lenders will experience a spike in business loan defaults and ultimately markedly higher loan losses.
There is some trepidation in the market already after business bank Judo Capital shocked investors last month when it warned that three bad loans amounting to about $75 million had soured.
The update, just a week out from the financial year's close, sent the stock plummeting 40 per cent, as investors fretted about the outlook and how businesses were managing the backdrop of higher rates and a tough operating climate.
Any increase in loan losses would follow a larger push into business lending by most of the major banks in the past two years, as margins came under sustained pressure in the mortgage market.
Fitch Ratings last month said it viewed the rapid expansion by large Australian banks into business lending "as a source of heightened asset-quality risk through the cycle", particularly if macroeconomic conditions weakened further.
"We expect impaired loan ratios to rise across the sector over the next 12 months against a backdrop of elevated interest rates, persistent inflation and a moderate uptick in unemployment," the report said.
Investors won't have to wait long, though, for insights into the operating climate and how businesses are faring. National Australia Bank, the nation's largest lender to businesses, is set to provide an update on July 30.
Investors and analysts have been invited to a briefing headlined by NAB's head of business and private banking, Andrew Auerbach. And with 13 months at the bank now under his belt, he'll outline a strategy refresh, and his expectations around credit quality and how businesses are placed to service their debts.
While household spending increased by more than economists expected in May, the data is being closely watched for any pronounced pullback in consumption.
The NAB business briefing marks a key event on the calendar and this year in particular, its timing is fortuitous.
Investors and analysts will also hear from other NAB leaders within the business division at the briefing, such as the head of agribusiness. At the same event last year, Auerbach was more of an observer and, having just started in the role, dialled in from Darwin where he was visiting customers.
This time he'll be fronting the show and attendees will want evidence he's across the detail.
The picture provided by NAB will give the market an insight into the broader banking sector and how it's placed to ride out a cycle of higher bad debts, albeit from a low base.
The latest data from the prudential regulator showed NAB ratcheted up its business lending market share to 21.9 per cent in the year ended May 31. But fierce rival Commonwealth Bank also increased its share over the period to account for 19.4 per cent of the market.
Westpac was in third spot with 16.6 per cent share, with ANZ the laggard at 13.4 per cent.
While credit quality is top of mind in business lending, an expected sharp drop in demand for home loans is already stirring competitive intensity.
Barrenjoey banking analyst Jon Mott has been closely monitoring dynamics over the past few weeks, observing that after CBA cut its mortgage pricing by five to eight basis points in June, ANZ and Westpac were quick to follow with their own reductions.
"We estimate ANZ cuts its mortgage rates by an average of about five basis points across the 60-80 per cent LVR [loan-to-value ratio] range. Westpac also moved its pricing, with cuts of circa 11 basis points in [the] 85 per cent LVR band (-16 basis points via the St George brand).
"WBC's cuts in the lower LVR bands were minor," Mott said, noting that after ANZ and Westpac moved, CBA then retaliated with a further pricing cut of about two basis points.
"We believe CBA is aiming to 'pincer' its peers. CBA has a scale and technology advantage over the other banks, and is generally preferred by most customers (as it holds the highest main financial institution and net promoter scores).
"As a result, pricing in line with peers is usually enough for CBA to win share," he added.
The cuts Mott is talking about essentially came through each respective bank increasing the available discount on the headline variable rates it has in the market.
Mott likened the competitive mortgage pricing tussle to seagulls fighting over the last chip. And we know how aggressive those birds can be when it comes to food!
If the industry descends into yet another home loan pricing war, net interest margins will come under renewed pressure and there will be questions about whether banks are writing loans that are priced below their cost of capital.
We aren't yet seeing the flood of cashback offers for mortgages, but credit growth is yet to markedly decline. With applications and loan pre-approvals falling away, though, banks have a good indication of how the pipeline is looking, and it's certainly softening.
Regarding Mott's analysis, much depends on how mortgage brokers apply for the customer's pricing and how banks effectively pitch for the loan. And it's important to remember lenders can tinker with their pricing levers at relatively short notice.
In one instance over the weekend, the cheapest pricing a broker could obtain among the big four banks for an owner-occupied mortgage with a chunky deposit of about 35 per cent emerged at ANZ at 6.07 per cent. That was followed by NAB at 6.09 per cent, while CBA's best deal under similar criteria was an annual interest rate of 6.13 per cent, in line with what Westpac was offering.
Cashback offers have already turned up on deposit and transaction accounts as some banks fight harder to win over customers this year. Westpac, for example, is offering personal customers $50 cashback offers across its brands, including St George, Bank of Melbourne, and BankSA, to lure them to open a transaction account alongside a savings account.
What's clear is that the tougher operating climate in the banking sector will test the mettle of executives who are seeking to navigate it sustainably. NAB's upcoming business briefing will provide some clues about just how difficult the months ahead are expected to be, while CBA reports full-year profits next month.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Joyce Moullakis
heading: Banking and Economy
